questions.prompt: When should you reach for React.lazy / next/dynamic?

choices.text: When a component is heavy (rich text editor, video player, complex chart, PDF viewer), rarely used (admin tools, settings modal), below the fold (not visible on first paint), or route-specific (one route only). Lazy-loading splits the component into a separate JS chunk loaded on demand — initial bundle stays small, non-critical code arrives when the user actually needs it. Don't lazy-load tiny components or above-the-fold content; the network overhead exceeds savings or hurts LCP. In Next.js App Router, each page is automatically a separate chunk.

questions.explanation: Heavy, rare, below-fold, or route-specific components. Not for tiny or critical-path components.

questions.prompt: Initial bundle is 800KB; bundle analyzer shows 300KB of that is a charting library used only on /dashboard. Fix?

choices.text: Lazy-load the charting library at the boundary where it's used. Move chart-using components behind `lazy(() => import('./Chart'))` (which internally imports the chart library); wrap in Suspense. The chart library now ships as a separate chunk loaded when /dashboard renders — homepage and other routes drop 300KB from their initial bundle. Combined with route-level splitting (Next.js does this automatically, or React Router with lazy routes), the bundle savings compound. Verify by re-running the bundle analyzer after the change.

questions.explanation: Lazy-load components that use heavy libraries; library ships in the lazy chunk, not initial bundle.
